title: A Home Away From Home
description:
A Home Away from Homeexamines the significance of Caribbean American mutual aid societies and benevolent associations to the immigrant experience, particularly their implications for the formation of a Pan-Caribbean American identity and Black diasporic politics.
At the turn of the twentieth century, New York City exploded with the establishment of mutual aid societies and benevolent associations. Caribbean immigrants, especially women, eager to find their place in a bustling new world, created these organizations, including the West Indian Benevolent Association of New York City, founded in 1884. They served as forums for discussions on Caribbean American affairs, hosted cultural activities, and provided newly arrived immigrants with various forms of support, including job and housing assistance, rotating lines of credit, help in the naturalization process, and its most popular function―sickness and burial assistance. In examining the number of these organizations, their membership, and the functions they served, Tyesha Maddox argues that mutual aid societies not only fostered a collective West Indian ethnic identity among immigrants from specific islands, but also strengthened kinship networks with those back home in the Caribbean. Especially important to these processes were Caribbean women such as Elizabeth Hendrickson, co-founder of the American West Indian Ladies’ Aid Society in 1915 and the Harlem Tenants’ League in 1928.
Immigrant involvement in mutual aid societies also strengthened the belief that their own fate was closely intertwined with the social, economic, and political welfare of the Black international community. A Home Away from Home demonstrates how Caribbean American mutual aid societies and benevolent associations in many ways became proto-Pan-Africanist organizations.

title: Chaconia Candles: Black Cake
description:
### Smells Like Rum and Raisin
Black Cake opens with a tantalizing mix of top notes featuring creamy coconut, caramelized sugar, and a sprinkle of warm cinnamon, creating an inviting introduction that enchants the senses. As the scent unfolds, the heart reveals the indulgent essence of rum and plump raisins, reminiscent of the beloved Caribbean dessert that is often the centerpiece of celebrations. The experience is beautifully anchored by the base notes of rich maple, aromatic nutmeg, warm clove, and allspice, evoking the comforting atmosphere of gatherings and family feasts.
**The Tradition Behind the Scent**
Black Cake (also called Rum Cake by other Caribbean islands) is the Caribbean’s signature celebration dessert during the holidays. It is rooted in plum pudding, reimagined with rum‑soaked fruits, browning, and island spices.
This candle is infused with natural essential oils including cinnamon leaf, vanilla absolute, styrax resin, clove bud, clove leaf, and eucalyptus.
**Note Profile**:
Top: Cinnamon
Middle: Rum, Raisin
Base: Allspice

title: Chaconia Candles: Christmas Morning
description:
### Smells Like Trini Traditions
Bright red currant, cool pine, and dry juniper capture the heartbeat of a Trini Christmas morning. Parang (and soca parang) on the radio, new curtains up, house painted, family in the kitchen, pastelles steaming, homemade bread on the table, and a glass of sorrel in hand, all Trini traditions on Christmas morning.
**The Tradition Behind the Scent**
Christmas morning in Trinidad & Tobago is a homecoming. In the months and even days before, houses get a deep clean, painted, and fresh curtains go up, a sign of care, pride, and a fresh start. At dawn, the speakers come alive with parang, the door swings wide for family and neighbors, and the kitchen fills with the smells of pastelles, homemade bread, and slices of black cake. Someone pours sorrel; stories and laughter flow.
This scent honors that feeling, bright, tart notes (red currant) nod to the ruby tang of sorrel; pine and juniper bring the crisp, green lift of holiday greenery and a bustling, open home. Whether you grew up with parang or you’re discovering it for the first time, Christmas Morning welcomes you to the table.
This fragrance oil is made with fragrance oils infused with essential oils, including eucalyptus, fir needle, lemon, cedar leaf, clove leaf, cedarwood, patchouli, and galbanum.
**Note Profile**:
Top: Red Currant
Middle: Pine
Base: Juniper

title: Chaconia Candles: Parang
description:
### Smells Like a Christmas Tree
Meet Parang, our bright-and-evergreen homage to a Caribbean Christmas. Sparkling lemon peel lifts a lush canopy of evergreen and fir, conjuring the crisp, resinous magic of a freshly trimmed tree; festive, cool, and joyously green. It’s the snap of citrus zest in the air, the clean hush of pine needles underfoot, and that unmistakable holiday feeling when the lights go on and the season officially begins. Fresh yet cozy, Parang fills your space with a classic Christmas-tree aura, kissed with island brightness.
**The Tradition Behind the Scent**
Named for Trinidad and Tobago’s beloved holiday tradition, Parang celebrates the joyful house‑to‑house serenades that signal the heart of the season. Bands gather with cuatro, maracas, and rich harmonies, moving from doorstep to doorstep as neighbors open their homes, share food and laughter, and let music spill into the night. This fragrance is one of togetherness: doors opening, glasses clinking, songs rising, and a warm island welcome.
_This candle is infused with natural essential oils, including fir balsam._

title: Chaconia Candles: Sorrel
description:
### Smells Like Spiced Hibiscus
Bring home the red‑ruby joy of a Trini Christmas. Inspired by the founder's island tradition of steeping hibiscus petals with citrus and warm spice, this fragrance channels the season of the holidays. It’s tart, bright, and cozy all at once, just like a cold glass of sorrel poured for family and friends.
**The Tradition Behind the Scent**
In Trinidad and Tobago, sorrel (pronounced “saw‑rel”) is more than a drink; it’s an invitation to togetherness and the holidays. Dried hibiscus petals bloom in hot water, orange peel adds a sunny lift, and cinnamon and clove wrap everything in warmth. The house fills with fragrance as it steeps, then some sugar for sweetness and chill, ready for sharing. This scent captures that memory: vibrant, welcoming, unmistakably Caribbean.
This candle is infused with natural essential oils, including cinnamon leaf and bark, orange, lemon, clove leaf, cassia leaf, and eucalyptus.
**Note Profile**:
Top: Orange Peel
Middle: Hibiscus (Sorrel)
Base: Cinnamon

title: Chef Jen Cuisine: Seasonings & Spices
description:
### _Our Seasonings are All-natural, Nut-free, Gluten -Free , No MSG, Vegan , No Additives. With your health in mind , we created 3  Salt-free blends . Our main priorities are Healthier alternatives & flavor.🌴_
* * *
### **🍃** **Jenesis Caribbean Green Seasoning**
_20 oz_
Unlock the essence of _🌴_ Caribbean cooking with every all-natural sprinkle. Introducing ourCaribbean green seasoning blend, now crafted in convenient dry form for the first time. This all-purpose blend is behind the vibrant, honey flavors that make Caribbean cuisine unforgettable. Now you can infuse your favorite dishes with the authentic, bold taste of tradition and bring the heart 🧡 of the Caribbean to your kitchen. _Great on all savory dishes._ 🥘
**Ingredients** 🌿
Garlic onion, thyme, bell peppers, cilantro, parsley, chives, ginger, celery, vegetable oil, and habanero flakes.
### **🍃** Koko Kreole - Creole Blend
Our Creole seasoning is a versatile spice mix. It works on meats 🍗, seafood 🦞, chicken, side dishes & Vegetables 🥗. It also tastes great in gumbos, stews , soups & Sauces 🍲 . _A pinch of this blend is the love_ 💛 _your kitchen has been missing_.
**Ingredients 🌿**
Hand blended from paprika, granulated onion, granulated garlic, oregano, basil, thyme , black pepper, white pepper, cayenne pepper and celery seed.
### **🍃** Vincy Spice - Curry Powder
_40 oz_
Dedicated to the land of my birth, St. Vincent & the Grenadines, where my love 💛 for cooking and Caribbean flavors begun. _This_ _rich blend_ _can be added to_ _meats_ 🍗, _chicken_, _seafood_ 🦞, _vegetables_ 🥗, _stews_, _curries_ _& as an all purpose seasoning.🌶️_
**Ingredients 🌿**
Coriander, turmeric, mustard fenugreek seed, cumin, cinnamon, red pepper flakes, ginger, black pepper, and cardamom.
### **🍃** Kookie Love - Steak & Thangs Seasonings
_50 oz_
Dedicated to my love 'Kirk Adams'. This blend adds a pronounced flavor to steak 🥩 , chicken, pork, and vegetables 🥗. _It's especially useful for seasoning burgers_ 🍔 _and beef dishes giving them an extra flavorful kick!_ This seasoning offers a truly bold and robust taste that is unlike any other. _Use as a dry rub or turn into a Marinade._
**Ingredients 🌿**
Sea salt, pepper, garlic, onion, canola oil, crushed red pepper, coriander, dill weed, rosemary, thyme.
### 🍃Resi Blendz Citrus Infused Salt
_5.50 oz_
Delightful citrus 🍊 flavor with just a hint of smokiness from the Smoked Sea Salt🧂. _This delectable seasoning can be used on fish chicken, pork , shrimp, stir fries, roasted vegetables and even on the rim of a margarita glass_ 🍸 _._
**Ingredients🌿**
Hand blended from sea salt, orange. black pepper, lemon, lime juice powder (organic tapioca Maltodextrin, Organic Lime Juice Concentrate, and Lime Oil), and ginger.

title: DWARE: Exfoliating Bath Sponge
description:
The DWARE exfoliating bath sponge will be the most valuable tool in your body care arsenal.
Our intricately knotted bath sponge is 💯 nylon and imported from Ghana, West Africa.  When used with bar soap🧼 or body wash, our exfoliating bath sponge creates a rich, thick lather and gently exfoliates the body.
At approx. 46in. long and 18in. wide, the DWARE exfoliating bath sponge is the ideal tool to thoroughly clean🫧 your body, especially hard-to-reach areas like your back.  Our bath sponge dries quickly, is great for traveling, and lasts for 18 -24 months.
Anyone can use the DWARE exfoliating bath sponge to turn their basic bath or shower into a luxurious experience.
**Use**
🧽Hand wash before your first use.
🧽Wet skin and lather with bar soap or body wash.
🧽Scrunch the sponge and wash your skin as you would with a washcloth.
🧽To clean your back, grab both ends of the sponge and move it across your back. from side-to-side.
🧽Rinse.
🧽Hang up to dry.
**Care**
Machine washable.
Line dry or dry on low heat.
**Caution:** Do not use on sensitive, broken, or damaged skin.

title: Flatbush Granola Company Room Diffuser
description:
The Brooklyn is Bae Reed Diffuser channels Brooklyn’s unmistakable energy with a scent that lingers like the buzz of stoop-side chats or the cool vibes of your favorite speakeasy.
Floral and citrus notes bring back easy layouts on a summer afternoon, and woody hints are reminiscent of backyard grills and firepits. This reed diffuser provides a continuous, subtle infusion of love for Brooklyn’s unique rhythm—effortlessly laid-back and endlessly cool.
**WARNING:**
**Flammable Liquid:** _Keep away from flames and hot surfaces. Do not light the reeds. Never use a flame to accelerate diffusion._
**Skin and Eye Irritant**:_Avoid contact with skin and eyes. In case of contact, rinse thoroughly with water. If irritation persists, seek medical advice._
**Ingestion Hazard:** _Do not ingest. If swallowed, do not induce vomiting. Seek immediate medical attention._
**Keep Out of Reach of Children and Pets****:** _Small parts may present a choking hazard. Essential oils can be toxic if ingested or applied to the skin without proper dilution._
**Use in Well-Ventilated Areas:** _To prevent potential overexposure to fragrance oils._
**Do Not Place on Wooden Surfaces or Furniture:**_Oil may cause damage to finishes & surfaces. Use a protective plate or coaster._
**Consult a Physician:** _If pregnant, nursing, suffering from any medical condition, or taking medication, consult a healthcare practitioner before use._

FLOR DE LA JARA is more than a brand — it’s the realization of a dream for us, Tiffany & Jorge, a Brooklyn-based couple united by a love of quality food and healthy living. Jorge is a third generation olive oil producer from Toledo, Spain, who grew up among olive trees, while Tiffany, a physician of Jamaican descent, brings her passion for nutritious products.

**ARBEQUINA**
**Elegant & Delicate**
Our Arbequina offers a well-balanced and harmonious fruity flavor—delicate and mild, with no overpowering bitterness or peppery notes. A Mediterranean favorite, perfect for everyday enjoyment.
**Aroma:** Fresh herbs, green apple, almond, and a hint of green banana.
**Pairings:**
An excellent choice for drizzling over salads, grilled vegetables, seafood, or steamed fish—and even for baking.
### **PICUAL**
**Versatile & Bold**
Our Picual embodies the strength and balance of Spain’s most iconic olive. Complex and expressive, it offers medium intensity with graceful bitterness and a lingering peppery finish.
**Aroma:** Green tomato, tomato plant, olive tree leaf, and a hint of aromatic herbs.
**Pairings:** Perfect for dipping artisan bread, finishing vibrant salads, or enriching the flavor of pasta, seasonal vegetables, and grilled meats.

title: Forgotten Lands: Volume 04 - Currents of Africa
description:
It’s quite striking how Caribbean island nations & the greater Americas contain cultural and rhythmic patterns often echoing those from across the Atlantic. This makes it impossible to examine the contemporary Caribbean without understanding the traditions that had traversed from West Africa to the West Indies.
Cover by Xavier Scott Marshall
**200 pages, first edition of 1000 copies**
**Featuring:**
_Andrae Green / Ania Freer / Dantaé Garee Elliott_/ _Don Brodie / Gherdai Hassell / Hadiya Sewer / Jodi Minnis / Joiri Minaya / Jorian Charlton / Kelly-Ann Bobb / Kiván Quiñones Beltrán / Lizania Cruz / Nicolas Derne / Rochelle Ward / Shameekia Shantel Johnson / Theodore Samuels / Xavier Scott Marshall_

title: Forgotten Lands: Volume 05- The Haunted Tropics
description:
For Volume 05 we’re examining the colonial origins that are still at work within and around the Caribbean today. _How might today’s coloniality of power present itself in your region and how do you address this in your practice?_  While we delve into this theme, it’s imperative to also consider the context of these matrixed layers of identity and the reality of our own displacement in the current post-globalized world.
Front cover by Shari Phoenix
Back cover by Guarionex Rodriguez Jr.
**Featuring:** _Daurius Figueira / Ed Maximus / Emann Odufu / Gervais Marsh / Guarionex Rodriguez Jr / Hew Locke / José Morbán / Leasho Johnson / Maelynn Ford / Maridelis Morales Rosado / Patience Williams / Pilar Castillo / Ricardo Miguel Hernández / Sandra Brewster / Savannah Lyons Anthony / Shari Phoenix / Sharmadean Reid / Steve Maldonado Silvestrini / Yelaine Rodriguez_

title: Forgotten Lands: Volume 06- Neo Carib Visions
description:
F
For volume 6, Forgotten Lands Publisher chose to center it around what Cuban historical novelist, critical essayist, and short story writer Antonio Benítez-Rojo once termed the ‘supersyncretic’ culture of the Caribbean - a rich tapestry of ever-evolving interwoven traditions and expressions.
Although syncretism emerges from the violent colonial histories and uprootedness of the Caribbean, each island-nation conjures different ways of expressing, coping, managing, and healing the fractures of conquest. Nonetheless, there still is a Pan-Caribbean identity that is perpetually evolving. This leads us to ask _what defines the ‘Neo-Carib’? What does our cultural identity look like today and where is it going?_
Cover: Bony Ramirez
**Featuring:** Amara King / Dr. Ashley James / Bony Ramirez / Cédrine Scheidig / Devin Osorio / Djali Brown-Cepeda / Frédéric Georges / Eliazar Ortiz / Janice Reid / Jard Lerebours / Julianny Ariza Vólquez / Karla Claudio / Louisa Marajo / Mackenzie Erica Luke / Dr. Margarita Lila Rosa / Milo Matthieu / Rachel Scott / Ricardo Ariel Toribio / Simon Benjamin / Suzanna Missenberger / Terrell Villiers / Torrijo Galeano / Vanessa Selk / Zulile Blinker

title: Terra-Tory: Soursop 430 Candle
description:
Stress has been a constant factor in my health journey. Taking on new experiences such as motherhood can be a great deal of stress. Ways I do that is by creating time for myself. It’s hard, I know because there isn’t enough time in the day. 24 hours just isn’t enough. So I created Soursop 430 to help de-stress after a long day of running a business and caring for a newborn. It contains relaxing notes of balsam pine, cedar leaf, and juniper berries. Hand poured locally in Brooklyn NY with non-toxic coconut wax and 100% cotton wicks for a clean burn.
Place in the office, bathroom, bedroom, kitchen, or even outside to create a cozy place for relaxing the mind and the body. My favorite thing to do while burning Soursop 430, is put my phone on Do Not Disturb, play any one of our TERRA-TORY Playlists on Spotify, and read a good book with a cup of tea.
When burning your candle for the first time, allow it to burn for 2 to 3 hours (but never more than 4), until the top layer of the wax has completely melted. This will prevent the wax from tunneling around the wick.
After each burn, see that the wick is centered in the wax. Ideally, we recommend keeping the wick trimmed to 1/4 inch _**at all times**_ to prevent the candle from emitting smoke or the wick falling into the wax.
This candle vessel and lid is 100% stainless steel. This vessel will get very hot. Keep out of reach of children and pets.
Our candles are simple: They're all made with 100% coconut wax, fine fragrance oils, and cotton-core wicks. The fragrances we use are free from parabens and phthalates and are never (ever) tested on animals.

title: Voices of Lefferts: Issue 7 (Flatbush Eats)
description:
The Voices of Lefferts Community History Project is proud to present a special issue of our journal on the topic of food as part of an initiative titled “Flatbush Eats: Food, Survival, and Celebration.”  Inspired by the New Deal era Federal Writers' Project (FWP), _Flatbush Eats_ aims to document the relationship between food and community in Flatbush-PLG. In the late 1930s, federal writers set out to collect stories and photos for a book to be titled "America Eats." Interrupted by the bombing of Pearl Harbor and the U.S. entry into the war, the project was abandoned and the book was never published. However, what remains of the FWP archive of stories, recipes, cookbooks, and photographs tells us not only about food per se but also about a nation in transition, still reeling from the impact of the Great Depression—a period of American history often invoked these past two years of COVID-19 in relation to our own times.
This first of two special issues on food features writing by writers with roots in Trinidad, Grenada, Ireland, the South Carolina Sea Islands, Tunisia, the Ukraine, and Palestine. Featured foods include roti, hot sauce, Jollof rice, harissa, maqluba, callaloo, and MacIntosh apples. All of these essays are suffused by memories of home, parents, grandparents even as they reflect our own, ever-changing, collective history in Brooklyn. They trace the myriad ways food serves as a portal to the nuance, beauty, challenge, pain, and resilience that characterize our community, especially since the advent of the pandemic in 2020. Also in this issue is our now regular art centerfold, this time aptly featuring photographs of the Maple Street Community Garden.

However, what remains of the FWP archive of stories, recipes, cookbooks, and photographs tells us not only about food per se but also about a nation in transition, still reeling from the impact of the Great Depression—a period of American history often invoked these past two years of COVID-19 in relation to our own times.
This second of two special issues on food features writing by writers with roots in Trinidad, Grenada, Ireland, the South Carolina Sea Islands, Tunisia, the Ukraine, and Palestine. Featured foods include roti, hot sauce, Jollof rice, harissa, maqluba, callaloo, and MacIntosh apples. All of these essays are suffused by memories of home, parents, grandparents even as they reflect our own, ever-changing, collective history in Brooklyn. They trace the myriad ways food serves as a portal to the nuance, beauty, challenge, pain, and resilience that characterize our community, especially since the advent of the pandemic in 2020. Also in this issue is our now regular art centerfold, this time aptly featuring photographs of the Maple Street Community Garden.

title: What's in the Cookie Tin?
description:
It's a universal experience! Generations of families from many countries and cultures share the common practice of repurposing the cookie tin.
This humorous picture book follows Olivia and Benjamin on their quest to eat what's inside the cookie tin. The siblings' experience at their grandparents' home sheds light on a multicultural phenomenon involving the cookie tins found in many homes worldwide. What do you think is in the cookie tin?
This book includes a delicious butter cookie recipe!

title: Zaire and Kenya's Kwanzaa Adventure: A Coloring and Activity Book
description:
Follow Zaire and Kenya throughout over **40** coloring and activity pages that celebrate Kwanzaa, family and the African Diaspora. This coloring and activity book is great for the entire family and will spark discussions about history, diversity and representation.
Large Size (8.5x11).
_Fun, informative and inspiring._
Great gift for children to learn more about Kwanzaa.
Over **40** coloring and activity pages highlighting the seven principles of Kwanzaa.
